results.possibleIdentity: Kirk Brainerd was a technical professional active in the late 1960s and 1970s, with expertise in mathematics, computer technology, electronics, and early artificial intelligence. He was a participant in the pioneering computer counterculture and social networking movements of that era. In 1973, Brainerd (associated with the name SMAX) was a long-distance participant in one of the first online computer conferences, which included other notable figures like Lee Felsenstein of the San Francisco-based community computing center Resource One. Brainerd was recognized in Ted Nelson's 1974 book 'Computer Lib / Dream Machines' for his work in Los Angeles, where he used computers to develop a registry of people with knowledge to share, with the goal of fostering altruism and mutual availability. Professionally, he collaborated with Gerald H. Shure at UCLA to develop 'MODELR' (Model building and model modification for instruction), a set of computer programs designed for student investigation of behavioral phenomena using natural language interaction, published in 1975. He is also associated with the San Francisco area and served or worked in a technical capacity related to the United States Ship Hancock (CVA-19), an attack aircraft carrier homeported in the San Francisco Bay Area during his period of activity.
